coredata.dc:title: An Eu-doped Zr-metal-organic framework for simultaneous detection and removal of antibiotic tetracycline
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Yinjun
Wang, Jiayu
Huang, Zhijun
Qian, Cheng
Tian, Yonghui
Duan, Yixiang
coredata.dc:description: Due to the widespread use of tetracycline (TC), there is an increasing demand for rapid detection as well as effective removal of residual tetracycline for environment and food safety. Herein, we report an Eu/Zr-MOF material that combines the high adsorption performance of water-stable Zr-MOF and the unique emission characteristics of Eu3+, which enables both sensing and adsorption functions for TC. Eu/Zr-MOF can be achieved facilely by doping Eu3+ into Zr-MOF via the traditional hydrothermal method. In aqueous medium, the Eu3+ preferentially coordinate with TC molecules, which in turn acts as an antenna for UV–Vis absorbance to enhance the characteristic fluorescence of Eu3+. In such way, TC can be specifically detected in a wide dynamic range (0.001–0.5 µg/mL) with a low detection limit (0.00092 µg/mL). Moreover, by taking advantage of the unsaturated character of ZrO centers, the Eu/Zr-MOF has a TC loading capacity as high as 289 mg g-1. As a consequence, based on the Eu/Zr-MOF, rapid detection and efficient removal of TC from food and environment was realized at once. The principle of design proposed in this work may find more applications to synthesize new platforms for simultaneous sensing and removal of specific pollutants.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Eu/Zr-MOF
Tetracycline detection
Antibiotic adsorption
Water purification
Fluorescence enhancement

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: TC recognition and adsorption mechanism of the Eu/Zr-MOF

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Key Research and Development Program of Shaanxi Province of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Key Technology Research and Development Program of Shandong

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation of Shaanxi Province
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Shaanxi Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research supported by Key Research and Development Program of Shaanxi Province of China ( 2019ZDLSF01-03 ), the National Key Research and Development Project ( 2018YFC1800903 ), National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21874095 ), Natural Science Foundation of Shaanxi Province (No. 2020JM-434 ), and the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation ( 2020M683538 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) SEM micrograph, (b) TEM micrograph, and (c) TEM-elemental mapping of the Eu/Zr-MOF.
(a) XPS, (b) FTIR, (c) XRD, (d) TG of Eu/Zr-MOF and Zr-MOF.
(a) UV–vis absorption and, (b) fluorescence spectra of Eu/Zr-MOF before and after addition of TC.
Response of the luminescence intensity at 615 nm of Eu/Zr-MOF toward different interferences including metal ions, organic molecules and other antibiotics.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of Eu/Zr-MOF in the presence of different concentrations of TC molecules (0–50.0 ug/mL) in the detection system. (b) fluorescence intensity of Eu/Zr-MOF versus different concentrations of TC.
Optimization of adsorption conditions of Eu/Zr-MOF: (a) media pH; (b) contact time and different initial TC (Experimental conditions: m = 10 mg; V = 50 mL).
The Langmuir (a) model and Freundlich (b) model isotherm fittings for TC on Eu/Zr-MOF at 25 ℃.
The O1s spectra of Eu/Zr-MOF (a) before and (b)after TC adsorption.
Parameters of adsorption isotherms for TC on Eu/Zr-MOF.

head.ce:title: An Eu-doped Zr-metal-organic framework for simultaneous detection and removal of antibiotic tetracycline

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center of Analytical Instrumentation, Northwest University, Xi’an, Shaanxi 710069, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Center of Analytical Instrumentation, Northwest University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Center of Analytical Instrumentation, Northwest University, Xi’an, Shaanxi, 710069, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Natural Functional Molecule Chemistry of Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry & Materials Science, Northwest University, Xi’an, Shaanxi 710069, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Natural Functional Molecule Chemistry of Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry & Materials Science, Northwest University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Natural Functional Molecule Chemistry of Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry & Materials Science, Northwest University, Xi’an, Shaanxi, 710069, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: College of Chemistry and Material Science, Northwest University, Xi’an, Shaanxi 710127, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Material Science, Northwest University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Due to the widespread use of tetracycline (TC), there is an increasing demand for rapid detection as well as effective removal of residual tetracycline for environment and food safety. Herein, we report an Eu/Zr-MOF material that combines the high adsorption performance of water-stable Zr-MOF and the unique emission characteristics of Eu3+, which enables both sensing and adsorption functions for TC. Eu/Zr-MOF can be achieved facilely by doping Eu3+ into Zr-MOF via the traditional hydrothermal method. In aqueous medium, the Eu3+ preferentially coordinate with TC molecules, which in turn acts as an antenna for UV–Vis absorbance to enhance the characteristic fluorescence of Eu3+. In such way, TC can be specifically detected in a wide dynamic range (0.001–0.5 µg/mL) with a low detection limit (0.00092 µg/mL). Moreover, by taking advantage of the unsaturated character of ZrO centers, the Eu/Zr-MOF has a TC loading capacity as high as 289 mg g-1. As a consequence, based on the Eu/Zr-MOF, rapid detection and efficient removal of TC from food and environment was realized at once. The principle of design proposed in this work may find more applications to synthesize new platforms for simultaneous sensing and removal of specific pollutants.

ce:section.ce:para: Tetracycline (TC), as a cheap antibiotic, is extensively used to treat bacterial infections in humans and animals [1]. However, due to the limited metabolic level of TC in the organisms, most of the TC ingested is excreted from the body and re-enter the environment [2]. Besides, TC is also widely used in animal husbandry and aquaculture, which further increases the TC level in the environment. However, TC is difficult to degrade in the surroundings, and even a low doses may have negative health effects [3]. Residual TC in the environment causes the emergence of antibiotic-resistant genes, which is likely to spread to other microbial populations, leading to super bacteria [4]. Therefore, it is an urgent problem to be solved on how to quickly and sensitively monitor TC and to remove it from the environment and food.

Currently, many analytical methods are available for quantitative determination of TC. The most common ones include liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) [5], high-performance liquid chromatography [6], capillary electrophoresis [7], thin layer chromatography [8], enzyme-linked immunoassay [9], etc., all showing high sensitivity and accuracy. However, the disadvantages of the time-consuming and complex sample preparation process, and the expensive equipment involved limit their widespread use. Sensor technique has become a new method of antibiotic detection with the advantages of high sensitivity, high selectivity, and easy operation. The current fluorescence sensing detection of TC is mainly based on graphene oxide [10], quantum dots [11], molecular imprinting [12], carbon nanoparticles [13], and other materials. Since the adsorption of pollutants is a low capital cost treatment method, it is most likely to be applied for practical environmental treatment. Therefore, there is growing attention to the study of tetracycline adsorption. Many adsorbents have been widely used, such as carbon nanotubes [14], activated carbon [15], smectite [16], oxide nanoparticles [17]. Currently, there are also some reports of simultaneous sensing and adsorption of TC [18,19]. They realize the detection by TC quenching fluorescence, and perform well in adsorption performance. If fluorescence can be enhanced by TC, the sensitivity may be further improved.

Metal-organic framework (MOF) has received increasing attention due to its high porosity, large specific surface area, adjustable structure, light and thermal stability, etc [20]. Due to the excellent luminescence properties of lanthanide ions (Ln3+), they are widely used in the field of sensing [21]. However, due to the forbidden f-f transition, the lanthanides are difficult to be excited or emit weakly in water; and due to the shielding effect by the outermost orbital electrons, the lanthanides ions are not sensitive to the environment change [22]. Therefore, a suitable ligand acting as an antenna is the key to opening the Ln-MOF fluorescence sensing [23]. In the "antenna effect", the organic ligand is initially excited by UV light, then the excitation energy is transferred from the organic ligand to the lanthanide ion, finally leading to fluorescence emission from the lanthanide ion [24]. Because of the strong ZrO bond, the MOF material with Zr as the main body has high stability for water, acid-base, and thermal energy [25]. In recent years, the application of Ln-MOF as a sensor has received much attention, especially for cations [26,27], anions [28,29], volatile organic solvents [30,31], small molecule pollutants [32,33], pH [34] and temperature [35]. In sensing processes, the main forms of luminescence response are luminescence quenching, luminescence enhancement and proportional fluorescence. In addition, the surface-functionalized Ln-MOF also shows excellent properties in terms of gas adsorption and separation [36,37]. However, Ln-MOF has been rarely reported on the adsorption of pollutants in water. Based on the diversity of Zr-based MOF ligands and the adjustable pore size, a variety of functional groups can be introduced for adsorption of specific pollutants. Zr-MOF has been widely used in the adsorption of heavy metals, pharmaceuticals, personal care products and organic dyes [38,39]. Combining the functions of two individual MOFs, new MOF materials can be obtained to realize some sensing function, and high adsorption efficiency in aqueous media. Such type of multifunctional materials provides a new way in the rapid monitoring and efficient management of environmental pollution.

In this work, we have developed a Eu3+ doped Zr- MOF to simultaneously detect and adsorb TC in environment and food samples. The MOF material was constructed by Eu/Zr metal centers and terephthalic acid methylammonium salt ligands. The Eu/Zr-MOF material was highly water-stable, and showed high absorption capacity for specific pollutants such as TC; meanwhile, the good fluorescence characteristics of Eu3+ was kept. As a result, TC in aqueous media can be sensed and efficiently absorbed simultaneously, just by using such a single MOF material. This idea of taking application as the goal and integrating multiple functional elements into a multifunctional platform in the design of functional materials provides a new route for environmental engineering applications.

Zirconium chloride (ZrCl4), Europium Nitrate (Eu(NO3)3), Terephthalic acid, Methylamine aqueous solution, N,N-dimethylformamide(DMF), TCs(Tetracycline, oxytetracycline, chlortetracycline, doxycycline), erythrocin, sulfapyridine, amoxicillin, streptomycin were Purchased from Shanghai Macleans Chemical Reagent Company. All the reagents and solvents (AR grade) were commercially available and used without further purification.

Eu/Zr-MOF was prepared by traditional hydrothermal method according to the reference with modification [40,41]. In detail, Eu(NO3)3 and ZrCl4 with the molar ratios of Zr4+: Eu3+ = 3–0.5. were dissolved into 15 mL of DMF containing aqueous terephthalic acid methylammonium salt (78.60 mg, 0.4 mmol), followed by addition of 1 mL deionized water. The above solution was mixed and stirred for 30 min. Then, the resulting solution was added to a 50 mL Teflon stainless steel reactor and heated at 120 °C for 18 h. The resultant was cooled to room temperature; Eu/Zr-MOF was obtained by centrifugal separation; and then was washed with DMF and methanol three times to remove the unreacted precursors. The washed Eu/Zr-MOF was vacuum dried at 60 °C for 8 h. The synthesis steps of Zr-MOF are the same as Eu/Zr-MOF as described above, except that Eu(NO3)3 was not added in the reaction system. The ligands of terephthalic acid methylammonium salt were prepared by dissolving terephthalic acid in methylamine (40 wt% in water), isolating the methylammonium salts via solvent removal under reduced pressure.

Crystalline structures were measured by X-ray diﬀraction (XRD, Bruker D8 advance). Surface composition and oxidation states of the element were characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, PHI 5000 Versa Probe III). Morphology and structure of the materials were investigated by field emission scanning electron microscope (SEM Hitachi, S-8100) and transmission electron microscope (TEM, Talos F200X). The optical properties of Eu/Zr-MOF were studied using Thermos Fisher UV–vis spectrophotometer and HORIB fluorescence spectrometer. The infrared spectra of ligand and TC were collected by BRUKER Fourier infrared spectrometer. The validation was performed with an UPLC-MS (Thermo QE ORBITRAP).

10.0 mg Eu/Zr-MOF was dispersed in deionized water to obtain a stock solution with a concentration of 1 mg/mL, which was stored in refrigerator for later use. The characteristic emission spectra of Eu/Zr-MOF were collected with 293 nm and 398 nm excitation light. Moreover, under the optimal conditions, the metal ions Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Pb2+, and the organic molecules such as glycine, leucine, glucose, urea, starch, and diphenylamine were discussed on respective spectral influence to Eu/Zr-MOF. At the same time, in the presence other antibiotics in the Eu/Zr-MOF aqueous solution, the spectral data were measured to evaluate the specificity of Eu/Zr-MOF to TC. The quantitative effect of TC on Eu/Zr-MOF was investigated by adding TC standard solutions of different concentrations to 0.5 mL of the deionized aqueous solution containing 1 mg Eu/Zr-MOF. This method can also be used to detect TC residues in actual samples of river water and pig muscle to assess its reliability.

UPLC-MS is a common method for detecting TC. The results of the spiked environmental samples detected by Eu/Zr-MOF were validated by UPLC-MS. Twenty river water samples have been taken for standard addition experiments, select four concentrations of 0.005, 0.05, 0.25, 0.5 ug/mL. Eu/Zr-MOF and UPLC-MS were used to detect the concentration of spiked samples. A linear regression equation was fitted to the two sets of data to evaluate the correlation. The parameters for UPLC-MS are listed in Tables S1.

The factors were investigated including the media pH (2−10), initial adsorbate concentration (10–100 mg/mL) and contact time (10–230 min). The performance of TC adsorption by Eu/Zr-MOF was evaluated when the pH was adjusted to 2–12 with dilute H2SO4 and NaOH. The adsorption kinetics was studied by adding 10 mg Eu/Zr-MOF to 50 mL TC solution with different initial concentration. The solution was placed on a constant temperature shaker for a fixed time, and a UV–Vis spectrophotometer was used to measure the TC concentration of the supernatant. The thermodynamic studies were performed at three temperatures of 25 °C, 35 °C, and 45 °C.

The regeneration ability of Eu/Zr-MOF was evaluated through adsorption and desorption experiments. In the desorption experiment, the used Eu/Zr-MOF was washed with distilled water and NaOH solution, then the regenerated Eu/Zr-MOF was collected by centrifugation; the next adsorption cycles were performed. The experiment was repeated three times at room temperature, and the adsorption amount of TC by Eu/Zr-MOF was calculated. Each experiment was performed with triplicate, and the adsorption capacity was calculated by the following equation,
q
t
=
(
C
0
−
C
t
) V
m
Where qt (mg g-1) is the adsorption capacity of TC on Eu/Zr-MOF, and C0 (mg/L) is the initial concentration of TC, Ct (mg/L) is supernatant concentration of TC at time t. V (L) is the volume of adsorption system, and m (g) represents the mass of Eu/Zr-MOF.

In order to optimize the luminescence and adsorption performance and balance the two functions, a series of Eu/Zr-MOF samples with different Eu and Zr ion molar ratios were prepared. MOF is characterized from two aspects: crystallinity and fluorescence intensity. We optimized the ratio of Eu3+ and Zr4+ (2:1; 1:1.5; 1:2; 1:3) in the process of synthesizing MOF. They are represented as Eu/Zr-MOF 1, Eu/Zr-MOF 2, Eu/Zr-MOF 3, Eu/Zr-MOF 4. As shown in Fig. S1, the XRD and fluorescence spectra of MOF at different ratios are displayed. According to Fig. S1a, we found that when the proportion of Eu3+ increases, the signal at 9.7° increases and the signal at 7.7° decreases. This may be because the addition of Eu3+ changes the structure of the MOF during the formation process. Through the BET test, as shown in Fig. S2 and Table S2, the higher the Eu3+ ratio, the smaller the surface area. The BET performance of Eu/Zr-MOF 3 and Eu/Zr-MOF 4 is basically the same, which shows that the effect of Eu3+ doping on the surface adsorption performance of MOF is not as small as possible. As shown in Fig. S1b, the higher the Eu3+ content, the stronger the fluorescence intensity. Zr4+: Eu3+=1.5:1 can take into account both luminescence and adsorption performance.

Eu/Zr-MOF was synthesized by the hydrothermal method. The surface morphology of the prepared Eu/Zr-MOF was characterized by SEM and TEM ( Fig. 1), where the crystalline form was clearly observed appearing as agglomerates in diameter of 10–30 nm. Compared with Eu/Zr-MOF, Zr-MOF (Fig. S3) showed very similar morphology, which indicates that the entry of Eu3+ did not destroy or change the morphology of Zr-MOF. Elemental composition and distribution including C, O, N, Zr and Eu were studied by TEM element mapping (Fig. 1c), which confirmed the presence of Eu3+ in the Eu/Zr-MOF. The Eu3d peak cannot be seen in Zr-MOF ( Fig. 2a). Is reflects the incorporation of Eu3+ into Eu/Zr-MOF, which was consistent with the results of TEM element mapping.

Eu/Zr-MOF and Zr-MOF show similar FT-IR spectra as shown in Fig. 2b, especially in the low frequency region, where 662 cm-1 and 769 cm-1 were attributed to the bond stretching and in-plane bending of ZrO, respectively. This can be explained by the similar coordination environments for Eu3+ and Zr4+, resulting in basically the same vibration modes relating to ZrO and EuO [18].

For the Eu/Zr-MOF and the pure Zr-MOF with the same kind ligands, they showed slightly different XRD diffraction peaks (Fig. 2c). Near the strongest peak at 7.7°, Eu/Zr-MOF had a small peak at 9.7°, while a small peak appeared at 12.3° for Zr-MOF. This suggests that after Eu3+ doping, the crystal structure of Zr-MOF was changed to some extent. Both of the two MOFs have a smaller peak at 25.9°. However, with low signal strength for Eu/Zr-MOF. This may be due to the fact that the main structure of Zr-MOF was remained upon the addition of Eu3+; on the other hand, due to the larger ion size of Eu3+ than Zr4+, the lattice expands and the overall crystal form tends to be disordered [41].

Thermogravimetric analysis shows that the weight loss could be divided into two successive stages (Fig. 2d). In the first stage (25–400 ℃), the guest solvent molecules were lost [42]; in the second stage (400 ℃–800 ℃), the framework was collapsed and the metallic oxide (ZrO2 and Eu2O3) was produced [43]. By comparison, Eu/Zr-MOF shows similar thermal stability to Zr-MOF [44]. The thermal stability of Eu/Zr-MOF is attributed to the very strong binding between Zr and O atoms in the ligand, which can be explained by the large electron density on the ligand that was polarized by Zr at high oxidation state [45].

UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded in the range 190–700 nm for Eu/Zr-MOF, Eu/Zr-MOF +TC and TC. As shown in Fig. 3a, TC alone has two strong absorptions at 275 nm and 359 nm without MOF participation; when Eu/Zr-MOF and TC are together, most of the TC is adsorbed, leading to the disappearance of the characteristic absorption peak of TC. At the same time, a new broad absorption peak at 398 nm appeared in the Eu/Zr-MOF-TC, which is due to the coordination of TC with Eu3+ [46]. These characteristics can be observed in Figs. 3a and S4. In Fig. 3b, when excited at 398 nm without TC, MOF has almost no characteristic emission of Eu3+. However, in the Eu/Zr-MOF-TC system, there is an obvious Eu3+ characteristic emission which is excited at 398 nm. The fluorescence emission bands at 579, 591, 615, 652 nm were attributed to the characteristic transitions of Eu3+ from 5D0 to 7F0−3 [47]. It further illustrates the successful coordination of Eu3+ in MOF. The peak at 520 nm was attributed to the fluorescence emission of TC [48]. It is worth noting that when the concentration of TC in the system increases, the characteristic fluorescence of Eu3+ also increases, especially the strongest fluorescence intensity at 615 nm. When Eu/Zr-MOF was combined with TC, the fluorescence emission of the Eu3+ ions was enhanced because of energy transfer from the excited TC transferred to Eu3+ leading to excitation of the latter [49]. As shown in Fig. S5, it can be seen from the fluorescence spectrum that Zr-MOF does not have the characteristic light of Eu3+ at 615 nm when excited at 293 nm or 398 nm in the absence or the presence of TC, respectively. Fluorescence enhancement was apparently observed after Eu3+ doping. In addition, Fig. S6 shows the long-term fluorescence stability of Eu/Zr-MOF in water and in a relatively wide pH range, which reflects its structural stability in complex aqueous environment. The spectral features of the Eu/Zr-MOF shows its potential as a new material for TC sensing. In order to optimize the reaction time, the fluorescence intensity of Eu/Zr-MOF measured after adding TC (Fig. S7). According to the sensing experiment within 15 min, it was found that the fluorescence intensity remains stable after 6 min. The optimal reaction time for the following experiment is 6 min. To prove that the Eu3+ is not leaching out in the aqueous solution. As shown in Fig. S8, the Eu/Zr-MOF supernatant has no Eu(NO3)3 absorption at 300 nm, which shows that Eu3+ ions are not leached from the Eu/Zr-MOF.

In order to explore the effect of metal ions and organic molecules on the fluorescence of Eu/Zr-MOF, the MOF excitation spectra were collected with the presence of a set of those substances (Fig. S4a). It was observed that the excitation at 293 nm was enhanced or quenched to some extent when these substances were present, whereas the excitation at 398 nm was not affected. This means that the emission spectrum of Eu/Zr-MOF was not interfered by the metal ions or organic substances, Therefore, we used the Eu/Zr-MOF to sense different kinds of antibiotics and explore the specificity of Eu/Zr-MOF to TC. As shown in Fig. S4b, among the different types of antibiotics including antibiotics including erythrocin(EM), sulfapyridine(SASP), amoxicillin(AMX), streptomycin(SM), doxycycline(DOX), chlortetracycline(CTC), and oxytetracycline(OTC), only tetracycline antibiotics (TC, OTC, DOX, CTC) have obvious fluorescence enhancement to Eu/Zr-MOF. The antibiotics except tetracyclines had a quenching effect on Eu/Zr-MOF at 293 nm, which may be due to energy competition. However, the non-tetracycline antibiotics did not respond at 398 nm ( Fig. 4), and thus Eu/Zr-MOF can specifically recognize TCs. According to the fluorescence properties of Eu/Zr-MOF, we choose excitation at 398 nm and emission at 615 nm to study TC sensing.

Based on the above discussion, the 615 nm fluorescence intensity (398 nm excitation) was used to realize the quantitative analysis of TC. As shown in Fig. 5a, fluorescence intensity of Eu/Zr-MOF at 615 nm grew with the increase of TC concentration. There is an excellent linear relationship in the concentration range of 0.001–0.500 µg/mL (y = 122795x+23025, R2 = 0.997) (Fig. 5b). The limit of detection (LOD) for TC was 0.00092 µg/mL, which was calculated as three times of the standard deviation (3σ, n = 12) of the blank test. The LOD value obtained is well below the residue limits established by the European Union and China. Besides, as shown in Table S4, the detection limit in this work is comparable to most of the previously reported TC recognizing sensors.

In addition to monitoring the water quality, the removal of residual TC in the water is also of great significance, as TC is difficult to degrade. In order to test the adsorption performance of the prepared MOF material for TC, the adsorption experiment carried out by using Eu/Zr-MOF. The pH value, TC concentration and contact time are the key factors affecting adsorption, and hence the three variables were optimized. The pH effect on the absorption capacity depends on the functional groups of the material's surface and TC molecular structure. [50]. The acid dissociation constants of TC depend on pH:pH > 7.7 (negative ions), pH < 3.3 (cationic ions), 3.3 < pH < 7.7 (Zwitter anions) [51]. As shown in Fig. 6a, when the pH is 2–3, the adsorption capacity drops sharply. Judging by the Zeta potential, this may be caused by electrostatic repulsion. At pH 4–8, the surface of TC is Zwitter anions, and electrostatic attraction promotes adsorption. The optimum pH was 5 to get the best adsorption capacity, and surface complexion may also be involved. At pH 8–12, the surface of TC is negatively charged. The amide bond in the Eu/Zr-MOF ligand can react with hydroxide ion, which may be the reason why the adsorption capacity and Zeta potential did not decrease in weak alkaline (pH 8–9). In addition to the electrostatic adsorption process, the π-π interaction of the aromatic structure in the TC molecule and the MOF ligand also plays a role in promoting adsorption at pH 4–10 [52]. The fact that the Zeta potential of Eu/Zr-MOF+TC is less than Eu/Zr-MOF+H2O at pH 12 indicates that there may still be an electrostatic attraction. It also explains why the adsorption capacity does not decrease much under extreme alkaline conditions. As showed in Fig. 6b, When the initial concentration was 100 ug/mL, the adsorption was rapid at the initial stage during 0–10 min; after that in 10–150 min, adsorption rate slowed down, while the adsorption capacity continued to increase until a equilibrium was reached at 150 min. Different initial concentrations cause different adsorption capacity, but the trend of the adsorption curve was basically the same. This may be attributed to the large adsorption surface area or adsorption sites at the beginning. After a period of adsorption, the adsorption sites were gradually depleted, causing competitive adsorption of TC molecules inside the MOF [53]. Finally, when the adsorption sites were all occupied, adsorption reached equilibrium.

To evaluate the adsorption characteristics, the pseudo-first-order kinetic model (Eq. (1)) and pseudo-second-order kinetic model (Eq. (2)) were applied to describe the adsorption kinetic of TC onto Eu/Zr-MOF,
(1)
ln
q
e
−
q
t
= ln
q
e
−
K
1
t
(2)
t
q
t
=
t
q
e
+
1
k
2
q
e
2
where q
t (mg g-1) is the amount of the adsorbate at time t, q
e (mg g-1) is the uptake of MOF at equilibrium, k
1 (min−1) is the pseudo-first-order rate constant, k
2 (g/mg/min) is the pseudo-second-order rate constant. According to Fig. S10, the experimental data of Eu/Zr-MOF and Zr-MOF were better fitted by the pseudo-second order model, as indicated by the R2 values higher than 0.99. Therefore, chemisorption dominated the TC adsorption. The amount of available binding sites limited the adsorption rate and adsorption capacity. The smaller crystallites size of the corresponds to a larger specific surface area and higher exposure of the binding sites, which is important for efficient adsorption. In order for a better understanding of the adsorption mechanism, Langmuir isotherm model (Eq. (3)) and Freundlich isotherm model (Eq. (4)) were used to further analyze the equilibrium adsorption characteristics of Eu/Zr-MOF,
(3)
C
e
q
e
=
1
k
L
q
m
+
C
e
q
m
(4)
ln
q
e
= ln
k
F
+
1
n
ln
C
e
where q
m (mg g-1) is the maximum adsorbed amount, q
e (mg g-1) is the uptake of MOF at equilibrium, k L is the Langmuir constant (L/g), k
F is the Freundlich constant (mg1−1/n L1/n g−1). As showed in Fig. 7 and Table 1, the adsorption of TC on Eu/Zr-MOF showed a better fit (R2 > 0.99) with Langmuir isotherm model based on the the experimental data, indicating multilayer adsorption on a heterogeneous surface at room temperature [54]. According to the Langmuir isotherm model, it is difficult to adsorb when 1/n > 2, while 0.1 < 1/n < 0.5 means a facile adsorption process [55]. The 1/n of this work was 0.54, showing that Eu/Zr-MOF has a good affinity for TC. The maximum TC adsorption capacity of Eu/Zr-MOF and Zr-MOF reached 289 mg g-1 and 299 mg g-1, respectively (Table 1). It may be that there are more ZrO bonds in Zr-MOF, which leads to stronger adsorption capacity. However, after doping with Eu3+, the adsorption capacity does not change much. The maximum adsorption capacity of Eu/Zr-MOF for TC was comparable and even higher than most adsorbents (Table S5). In addition, as shown in Fig. S9, the adsorption capacity of Eu/Zr-MOF increased with increasing temperature, indicating the endothermic nature of TC adsorption on the MOF [51].

The repeatability of the adsorbent is crucial for practical applications. As shown in Fig. S11, there was a sight to slight of the TC adsorption capacity with the increasing number of adsorption cycles. After the fourth cycles, the adsorption capacity was 225 mg g-1, still satisfying the treatment of TC. Thus, the prepared adsorbent was robust for repeat use to remove TC in water. We evaluated the crystal structure and morphology of the regenerated Eu/Zr-MOF, as shown in Fig. S12. Compared with the original Eu/Zr-MOF, the XRD and SEM of the regenerated Eu/Zr-MOF did not show any obvious changes. This phenomenon further supports the regeneration ability of Eu/Zr-MOF.

ce:section.ce:section-title: TC recognition and adsorption mechanism of the Eu/Zr-MOF
ce:section.ce:para: In the absence of TC, the Eu/Zr-MOF system can absorb light only at 245 nm and emit red light. In this process, the MOF ligand acted as an Eu3+ antenna, which facilitates the excitation of Eu3+ through LMCT and the emission of a characteristic spectrum. The TC introduced in the MOF can coordinate with Eu3+ to act as an antenna receive light. The detection system had obvious absorption near 398 nm, and with the increase of TC concentration, the characteristic emission of Eu3+ at 615 nm increased, as a result of stronger antenna effect [46]. However, for the Eu3+ alone in water, rather those in MOF materials, only weak emission was observed, due to radiationless decay by the vibrational motion of the surrounding water molecules [48]. It was hypothesized that TC concentration in the solution affect the number of water molecules (NH2O) in the Eu/Zr-MOF coordination domain [56], which is consistent with our experiment results and the above assumptions (Table S6).

The mechanism of TC adsorption was also partly revealed by XPS spectra. The peaks of O1s for Eu/Zr-MOF before and after TC adsorption as shown in Fig. 8, the OH peak appeared at 533.6 eV, which was attribute to TC, and the area ratio of the metal oxygen single bond (MO) peak before and after the adsorption of TC decreased from 29.44% to 22.18%, which is attributed to the hydrogen-bonding interactions between COOH/OH of TCs and NH2/COOH of Eu/Zr-MOF [18,57]. In the absorption process, the TC molecules first entered the outer surface of Eu/Zr-MOF, then diffused to the inner pore, and finally was adsorbed. Moreover, ZrO in Eu/Zr-MOF is characteristic of Lewis acid and can weakly combined with the alkaline amine groups in TC, which facilitates adsorption [43].

In order to assess the reliability of Eu/Zr-MOF for practical applications, river water and pork was selected as two actual samples for adsorption experiments. The actual sample processing is consistent with the previously reported method [58,59]. The experimental results are shown in Table S3. The recoveries were in the range 93.16–104.16% with relative standard deviation (RSD) of TC sensing systems was < 8.38% for all samples. The results satisfy the testing requirement, therefore the proposed Eu/Zr-MOF can be used as a sensor for detecting TC in actual samples.

Different concentrations of TC were detected by the two methods with largely consistent results, showing good correlations (Fig. S13). The obtained linear regression equation was: y = 0.9542x-0.0032 (R2 = 0.9738，n = 20), indicating that the TR-FRET analysis was accurate and reliable.

In summary, a Eu/Zr-MOF material was synthesized by using a simple hydrothermal method, which exhibited outstanding performance for sensitive detection and effective removal of TC. The Eu3+ doping and strong metal-ligand binding of ZrO endow the MOF material with unique spectral characteristics and structural stability in the process of detecting TC in aqueous sample. Meanwhile, the competitive TC absorption in the MOF material led to complete, coordination around the Eu3+, which avoids the interference from water molecules. The embedding of Eu3+ in the MOF enhanced its characteristic fluorescence intensity, thus resulting in a lower detection limit for TCs. It can specifically distinguish TCs with a wide sensitivity range (0.001–0.5 µg/mL) and a low detection limit (0.00092 µg/mL), and works very well for practical applications with high recoveries (93.16–104.16%). In addition, the Eu/Zr-MOF exhibits high stability and excellent TC adsorption capacity as high as 289 mg g-1 in aqueous solution. Therefore, the work provides a new method for sensitive detection and effective removal of antibiotics in food and the environment.

Yinjun Li: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - original draft preparation; Jiayu Wang: Data curation, Validation; Zhijun Huang: Writing - review & editing; Cheng Qian: Visualization; Yonghui Tian: Visualization, Writing - review & editing; Yixiang Duan: Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research supported by Key Research and Development Program of Shaanxi Province of China (2019ZDLSF01-03), the National Key Research and Development Project (2018YFC1800903), National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21874095), Natural Science Foundation of Shaanxi Province (No. 2020JM-434), and the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2020M683538).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jece.2021.106012.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:

.
